content.full_text:
CITY OF BURBANK
OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY
275 E. OLIVE AVE. • P.O. BOX 6459 • BURBANK, CALIFORNIA 91510-6459
(818) 953-9614 • FAX (818) 953-3136                                    JOSEPH W. FLETCHER
                                                                        CITY ATTORNEY

September 16, 1992


Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814


Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     The purpose of this letter is to request formal written
advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and Section
18329 of the Regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission on behalf of Michael Hastings, a member of the Burbank
City Council. I am the City Attorney for the City of Burbank and
have been authorized by Mr. Hastings to make this request
relating specifically to Mr. Hastings' duties under the Fair
Political Practices Act.

     On October 13, 1992, Walt Disney Studios will be coming
before the City Council seeking approval of the Disney Master
Plan, a proposal to demolish 249,626 square feet of offices,
studio production facilities and supporting uses and to
construct, in two phases, 2,059,989 square feet of offices,
studio production facilities and supporting uses, including a
helistop and a child care facility on their current site. Walt
Disney Studios is currently located in the Burbank Media District
on a 44-acre campus and is developed as a film studio with
1,071,809 square feet of offices, production facilities and
support services.

     As background, in January 1991, the City of Burbank adopted
the Media District Specific Plan (MDSP) which is a comprehensive
growth control plan governing all new development in the Media
District. Adoption of the MDSP marked the culmination of a six
year public process to develop a comprehensive growth control
plan for the Media District. As a consequence of the MDSP, the
City added to its Zoning Code a Media District Overlay Zone,
establishing new density limitations, height restrictions,
setback requirements and other guidelines for new development in
the Media District.

     Disney is seeking to be rezoned from MDM-1 (Media District
Industrial) and MDM-3 (Media District General Business) zones to

08/19/92
E:\WP51\DOCS\CA-DOCS\HASTings-SDU.sdu                                  Page 1


printed on recyclable paper

PD (Planned Development) zone. Although the current project is
consistent with the MDSP, a Planned Development Zone is being
sought because the project includes a vesting tentative map, a
vacation of air rights over a portion of a City street for a
pedestrian bridge, a development agreement, a request for a child
care center, a helispot and building heights over 75 feet. These
requests would typically require a separate approval, conditional
use permit or administrative use permit in the absence of a
Planned Development Application.

To the east of the proposed project is a residential
community known as the Rancho. Councilmember Hastings owns and
resides in a home approximately 700 feet from the border of the
Disney project, in the Rancho neighborhood. Accordingly,
pursuant to regulation 18702.3, Councilmember Hastings has a
conflict of interest if the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable effect of $10,000 or more on the fair market value of
the house (or will affect the rental value of the property by
$1000 or more per 12 month period).

We tentatively concluded that the project would not have a
material financial effect on Councilmember Hastings' property.
We based this decision on (1) the fact that since Disney is
already located on the site, the project would not change the
character of the neighborhood; (2) the distance of Councilmember
Hastings' property from the project site and its relationship to
the project site; (3) the fact that the project is consistent
with current zoning regulations; and (4) the conclusion of the
Draft EIR for the project that any impacts of the project would
be mitigated to a level of insignificance through the required
adherence to the already adopted standards and regulations of the
MDSP.

For example, while the Draft EIR states that the proposed
project would result in a change in the intensity of land use, it
concludes that there are no significant adverse impacts because,
pursuant to the MDSP, Disney is responsible for the protection
and preservation of adjoining residential neighborhoods through
development setbacks, landscape buffering, traffic and parking
intrusion prevention, building height restrictions and public
service improvements. The Draft EIR did identify some negative
impacts including noise, increased traffic and related air
quality degradation. With the exception of the cumulative effect
on air quality, all other impacts were determined to be
insignificant. Finally, the Draft EIR states that the project
will result in an improvement in the visual character of the
project site.

Nevertheless, we realize that some residents do not like to
be near new construction of any sort. Furthermore, due to the
myriad factors involved, we decided to seek the assistance of a

08/19/92
E:\WP51\DOCS\CA-DOCS\HASTings.SDU:adu                                    Page 2

real estate agent familiar with the Rancho community to give us
his professional assessment of the financial effect, if any, the
project would have on Councilmember Hastings' property. The real
estate agent also concluded that the project would not have a
material effect on Councilmember Hastings' property. For
reference, we have attached a copy of his opinion to this letter.

Please provide us with your opinion on whether or not
Councilmember Hastings may participate in the decision on the
Disney Master Plan. If you have any questions, or require
additional information regarding this request, please do not
hesitate to contact me. Your immediate attention to this matter
is greatly appreciated, as it is scheduled to be heard by the
City Council on October 13, 1992. Please contact me if you feel
you will not be able to provide us with advice by this date.

Very truly yours,

Joseph W. Fletcher

08/19/92
E:\WP51\DOCS\CA-DOCS\HASTings.SDU:sdu                                Page 3

September 16, 1992


Joseph W. Fletcher
City Attorney of Burbank
275 E. Olive Avenue
Burbank, CA 91510

RE: Your request for an opinion on the financial impact of the
    Disney Master Plan on Councilmember Hastings' property located
    at 611 South Orchard Drive in the City of Burbank.

Dear Mr. Fletcher:

As you know, I am a licensed real estate agent in the state of
California and I am currently working to meet the requirements for
a California state brokers license.   My job duties include
determining property values, marketing residential real estate and
negotiating purchase contracts between buyers and sellers, all
primarily in the City of Burbank.

You have asked for my opinion regarding whether or not the Disney
Master Plan decision will have a material financial effect on
Councilmember Hastings' residential property located at 611 South
Orchard Drive in the City of Burbank.

Currently, the Walt Disney Studios is located on a 44-acre site in
the Burbank Media District and consists of 1,071,809 square feet of
development.  Pursuant to the Disney Master Plan, Disney proposes
to add 2,059,989 square feet of offices, studio production
facilities and supporting services, including a helistop and a
child care facility.    Councilmember Hastings' property is
approximately 700 feet from the project site.

I have concluded that the Disney Master Plan will not change the
value of Councilmember Hastings' property.  I have studied the
Media District Specific Plan ("MDSP"), the Disney Master Plan and
its Draft Environmental Impact Report, and as a real estate
professional I feel that the standards and mitigation measures
included in these plans will neither enhance nor destroy the Rancho
neighborhood, but rather simply keep it constant.




                                                    RE/MAX Media City Realty
                                                    3500 W. Olive Ave., #110
                                                    Burbank, California 91505
                                                    Office: (818) 846-3500, 563-0323
                                                    Fax: (818) 953-4155
                                                    Each Office Independently Owned and Operated

Presently, Disney's presence has been unobtrusive to the Rancho
area and the MDSP has set standards that will ensure Disney stays
within the already established guidelines designed to keep any
change at insignificant levels. The MDSP, the Disney Master Plan
and the DEIR are the three key factors which helped me come to a
decision. These plans were drafted in order to preserve a unique
community here in the Burbank area and it is my feeling that this
will be done. The Rancho area and Councilmember Hastings' property
will remain status quo.

I have based my conclusion on the following analysis. First, I
started with the factors considered when evaluating property value.
These factors are:

I.     Market Conditions.
       A.     Recently sold properties.
       B.     Availability of similar properties
              (supply and demand).
       C.     Availability of financing.

II.    Location.
       A.     General (area of city).
       B.     Proximity to railroad, flight pattern, busy street,
              freeway, industrial.
       C.     Schools.

III.   Physical Aspects of the Structure.
       A.     Size, lay-out, flow, condition, amenities, view and
              noise pollution.

IV.    Aesthetic Value.
       A.     "Curb appeal".
       B.     Character of the neighborhood.

Second, I applied these factors to the question at hand. The
market conditions of the Rancho area will not be effected by the
Disney proposal for two reasons: The first is, as detailed in the
Draft Environmental Impact Report (DEIR), the project must adhere
to the standards and regulations adopted as part of the Media
District Specific Plan. The second is that because of the R-1-H
Zoning, which is unique in the City of the Burbank, horse property
will always be in demand.

The next factor I considered is the general location of the
property and its proximity to the actual project. After reviewing
the proposed plan, I have concluded that the project should have no
effect on Councilmember Hastings' residence because of the distance
between the residence and the project site. Furthermore, the
subject property is not located on a main thoroughfare which makes
possible changed traffic conditions and noise pollution problems a
non-issue.

The physical aspects of the structure; size, lay-out, flow,
condition and amenities, cannot be changed by such outside forces.
The view will not be changed because the residence is not within
sight of the project. Noise pollution is a potential problem when
there is construction and a resulting greater use intensity,
however, according to the DEIR it has been determined to be
insignificant.

When realtors use the term "curb appeal" they are referring to the
appearance of the property from the street. The Disney plan cannot
change this quality because it is something that is solely unique
to the structure itself. The character of the neighborhood as well
as Councilmember Hastings' property will be preserved by the
already adopted Media District Specific Plan which the Disney
Master Plan must adhere to. The standards set by the MDSP will
ensure that there is no significant change to the Rancho
neighborhood.

Sincerely,

[signature]
George F. Paul
Realtor
Remax Media City Realty
